title: Executive Order 7450—Excusing From Duty on Wednesday, September 23, 1936, Until 1 p.m., Employees of the Executive Departments, Establishments and Other Agencies of the Federal Government in the District of Columbia, Who Desire to Show Honor to the Veterans of the Civil War Upon the Occasion of the Parade of the Grand Army of the Republic

flags.justification: A narrow, one-time administrative housekeeping action excusing employees from duty for a few hours; no concentration of any kind.
The order itself specifies who is excluded from the excusal ('special public reasons' or inconsistency with existing law), a reasonably clear, bounded scope.
Single purpose: a one-time administrative excusal for a specific event.
No compliance regime is created by a one-time administrative excusal.
No compliance regime exists for an incentive structure to attach to.
A one-time, single-day action with no ongoing authority requiring reauthorization.
No existing oversight mechanism is removed.
No compliance regime is created by this order.
About as minimal a structural action as exists in this corpus: a one-time, few-hours administrative courtesy tied to a specific historical commemorative event.
No second agency or mandate is implicated.

The order's carve-out for employees excluded from the excusal defines who benefits from a one-time courtesy grant rather than creating an exemption from a regulatory scheme; the flag's concept does not map onto this kind of action.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 0/10 = 0.0%. A genuinely trivial order — excusing D.C.-area federal employees for a few hours to attend a Civil War veterans' parade — and it scores at the floor, exactly as the negative-class validation sample is designed to demonstrate.
